steps.prompt: How many finite verbs are in this sentence?

hints.One: Look again — 'video', 'probo' (in 'proboque'), and 'sequor' are all finite verbs
hints.Four: 'meliora' and 'deteriora' are adjectives, not verbs — only 'video', 'probo', and 'sequor' are finite

hints.vinco, vincere, vici, victum: These are the principal parts of 'vincere' (to conquer)
hints.vivo, vivere, vixi, victum: These are the principal parts of 'vivere' (to live)
hints.venio, venire, veni, ventum: These are the principal parts of 'venire' (to come)

hints.to test: 'to test' can be 'probare' secondarily, but the primary meaning here is 'to approve'

hints.prohibeo, prohibere, prohibui, prohibitu: These are the principal parts of 'prohibere' (to prevent)
hints.prodo, prodere, prodidi, proditum: These are the principal parts of 'prodere' (to betray)
hints.propono, proponere, proposui, propositum: These are the principal parts of 'proponere' (to set forth)

steps.target: It is a deponent verb — passive in form, active in meaning
choices: It is a deponent verb — passive in form, active in meaning
It is in the passive voice

hints.It is a defective verb: Defective verbs lack certain forms — 'sequor' has all forms, but is deponent (passive form, active meaning)
hints.It is in the passive voice: 'sequor' looks passive but is deponent — it has active meaning despite passive morphology
hints.It is subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'sequar' — 'sequor' is present indicative deponent

hints.seco, secare, secui, sectum: These are the principal parts of 'secare' (to cut)
hints.quaero, quaerere, quaesivi, quaesitum: These are the principal parts of 'quaerere' (to seek)
hints.loquor, loqui, locutus sum: These are the principal parts of 'loqui' (to speak) — another deponent verb

translation: I see the better things and approve them; I follow the worse
